articleSummary: The article analyzes the paradox of Google’s self-driving cars having higher accident rates than human drivers, though almost all collisions are caused by humans unfamiliar with the robots' rigid law-abiding behavior. It argues that absolute rule-following—while minimizing liability—creates safety hazards (e.g., inability to merge or avoid rear-enders), invoking the trolley problem for ethical and legal edge cases. The author proposes a three-tiered perception system allowing flexible, context-aware rule-breaking to avoid accidents, framing the tone as a solution-oriented critique aimed at a general tech and policy audience. Recurring metaphors include “robots drive like octogenarians” and the trolley problem as a central dilemma.
